title: Data Center AI Growth Faces Challenging Bottlenecks
gist: Data center AI growth is constrained by four compounding supply-chain bottlenecks: TSMC advanced foundry/CoWoS packaging (plus ABF film and ASML lithography), HBM/DRAM memory, data-center electrical power, and optical-interconnect lasers. These bottlenecks, plus geopolitical concentration in Taiwan and South Korea, are likely to persist for years.

insights.claim: Anthropic is the leading frontier model provider with an annualized revenue run rate of roughly $47 billion, with OpenAI close behind at roughly $30 billion.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Frontier-model revenue scale confirms AI compute demand is backed by real ARR, supporting continued capex into the constrained supply chain.
insights.quote: Anthropic, the leading frontier model provider with an annualized revenue run rate (ARR) of ~$47 billion with OpenAI close behind at ~$30 billion
insights.locator: AI is rocketing ahead

insights.claim: Google Gemini processes over 3.2 quadrillion tokens per month, up 7x year over year, even though Google does not break out Gemini revenue.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: 7x YoY token growth signals demand elasticity for inference compute far beyond what disclosed revenue reveals; a leading indicator for accelerator and memory demand.
insights.quote: Google Gemini processes over 3.2 quadrillion tokens per month (Google I/O, May 2026), which is up 7x year over year
insights.locator: Anthropic, the leading frontier model provider

insights.claim: Broadcom CEO Hock Tan stated that customer demand for AI compute is insatiable through 2028, implying multi-year demand visibility for AI silicon.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: A named multi-year demand runway through 2028 underpins order backlogs at custom-ASIC and networking suppliers like Broadcom.
insights.quote: Hok Tan, CEO of Broadcom, said on CNBC June 24 that customer demand is insatiable through 2028
insights.locator: Anthropic says it could grow faster with more compute

insights.claim: OpenAI has developed its own AI accelerator, called Jalapeño, to deliver more throughput with less power and silicon and to add to its compute resources.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Custom in-house silicon by frontier labs erodes Nvidia's TAM at the margin and signals vertical integration to escape compute scarcity.
insights.quote: OpenAI has developed its own AI accelerator, Jalapeño, to deliver more throughput with less power and silicon
insights.locator: Anthropic says it could grow faster

insights.claim: Older Nvidia GPUs remain fully utilized because they provide incremental compute in a supply-constrained environment, and frontier labs have learned to run models on any AI compute they can buy or rent at scale.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Persistent utilization of legacy GPUs extends the depreciation and resale value of prior-gen hardware, a positive for installed-base economics.
insights.locator: The major players are buying or renting AI compute

themes.theme: Bottleneck 1: TSMC Foundry, CoWoS Packaging, ABF and ASML

insights.claim: Almost all data-center AI compute is manufactured by TSMC, giving it an almost-monopoly because it has the advanced processes, the volume, and the advanced packaging to integrate multiple chiplets on interposers and substrates.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: TSMC's near-monopoly on AI compute manufacturing makes it the single most critical chokepoint and a structural pricing-power beneficiary.
insights.quote: It's an almost-monopoly for data center AI.
insights.locator: Bottleneck #1 – Advanced Foundry and Packaging Capacity

insights.claim: TSMC's revenues are expected to grow 4x from 2023 to 2028 while margins trend upward into the mid-60% range, notably without the radical price hikes memory companies have imposed.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: 4x revenue growth with expanding mid-60s margins and pricing restraint signals durable, defensible earnings power versus boom-bust memory peers.
insights.quote: TSMC's revenues are expected to grow 4X from 2023 to 2028.
insights.locator: TSMC's revenues are expected to grow 4X

insights.claim: TSMC raised its 2030 global semiconductor market forecast to $1.5 trillion (up from $1 trillion), with AI expected to be 55% of the total, smartphones 20%, and automotive 10%.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: AI at 55% of a $1.5T 2030 TAM reframes the whole semi market as AI-led; sizing tool for allocating across the AI supply chain.
insights.quote: AI is expected to be 55% of the total market, followed by 20% for smartphones and 10% for automotive.
insights.locator: Recently TSMC raised its outlook

insights.claim: TSMC's CoWoS packaging capacity, essential for AI compute, is growing at an 80% CAGR from 2022 to 2027, while AI accelerator wafer demand is growing more than 11x over the same period.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Wafer demand (>11x) far outrunning packaging capacity (80% CAGR) quantifies the persistent CoWoS shortage that gates accelerator shipments.
insights.quote: Its CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate) packaging capacity, essential for AI compute, is growing at 80% CAGR from 2022 to 2027.
insights.locator: TSMC's CEO CC Wei says

insights.claim: TSMC CEO CC Wei says it will be a long time before the company can meet customer demand, despite heavy investment in multiple new fabs and packaging facilities in the USA and Taiwan.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: The CEO explicitly signaling multi-year unmet demand means the supply gap, and its pricing tailwind, is unlikely to close soon.
insights.quote: it will be a long time before we can meet customer demand
insights.locator: TSMC's CEO CC Wei says

insights.claim: TSMC is outsourcing some CoWoS packaging to ASE and Amkor to help meet demand, and is developing CoPoS (Chip-on-Panel-on-Substrate) packaging using glass core substrates to cut costs and boost wafer utilization.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Packaging outsourcing directly benefits OSATs ASE and Amkor; the CoPoS/glass-substrate shift is a next-gen packaging theme to track.
insights.locator: TSMC is developing CoPoS

insights.claim: TSMC is expected to prioritize Nvidia, AMD, Broadcom, and other major strategic customers, and it rigorously validates customer wafer orders against market data to confirm demand is realistic.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Allocation priority to the top few customers entrenches incumbents and disadvantages smaller AI-chip startups competing for wafers.
insights.locator: TSMC is expected to prioritize

insights.claim: The ultimate limiter on even TSMC's growth is people, not capital or equipment, capping how fast advanced capacity can ramp.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: If skilled labor, not money, is the binding constraint, then capex announcements overstate how quickly the bottleneck can be relieved.
insights.quote: But even TSMC can only grow so fast — people are the ultimate limiter.
insights.locator: TSMC has done an amazing job

insights.claim: There are few good alternatives to TSMC: Intel and Samsung have leading-edge nodes but far less capacity and weaker packaging, GlobalFoundries abandoned finFET and tops out at 12nm which is useless for data-center AI, and SMIC lags due to US lithography export controls.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: The absence of a credible near-term second source underpins TSMC's pricing power and frames the multi-year opportunity for anyone who catches up.
insights.locator: There are few good alternatives to TSMC

insights.claim: SMIC can build trailing-edge processes that are far less power-efficient but are used by Huawei to build performance-competitive AI compute, since electricity is abundant in China.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: China trading power efficiency for raw performance means export controls don't fully neutralize its AI-compute ambitions; a geopolitical wildcard.
insights.quote: SMIC is able to build trailing-edge processes (much less power efficient, but not a concern in China where electricity is abundant) that Huawei uses to build performance-competitive AI compute
insights.locator: SMIC (Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corporation)

insights.claim: Major players are likely to shift lower-volume or lower-complexity chips to Intel and Samsung to free up TSMC allocation, with rumors that Google ordered millions of TPUs from Intel or Samsung and Trump saying Apple will use Intel (unconfirmed by Apple).
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: A spillover of overflow orders could make Intel or Samsung Foundry a viable #2, a potential re-rating catalyst if the TPU/Apple rumors are confirmed.
insights.quote: Rumors are Google has given Intel (or Samsung?) orders for millions of TPUs
insights.locator: Given the huge demand

insights.claim: All foundries except SMIC are bottlenecked by ASML of the Netherlands, the only supplier of advanced lithography equipment, though ASML does not appear to be the binding constraint on capacity expansion.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: ASML remains a single-source chokepoint but is explicitly framed as not the current binding constraint, tempering the litho-shortage narrative.
insights.locator: All foundries, except SMIC, are bottlenecked

insights.claim: Japan's Ajinomoto makes over 95% of the global supply of ABF (Ajinomoto Build-up Film) used to connect GPU dies to HBM stacks, raised prices 30% in 2026, and projects a supply gap of over 20% in 2027.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: A single supplier of 95% of ABF raising prices 30% with a 20%+ 2027 shortfall is a hidden, high-leverage chokepoint on all advanced AI packaging.
insights.quote: It raised prices 30% in 2026, and projects a supply gap of >20% in 2027
insights.locator: All foundries are also bottlenecked by ABF

insights.claim: The DRAM market has consolidated from dozens of manufacturers in the 1990s to just three dominant players today: SK hynix, Samsung, and Micron.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: A three-firm oligopoly gives DRAM makers pricing discipline during the AI shortage, unlike the fragmented market of prior cycles.
insights.locator: In the 1990s there were dozens of manufacturers

insights.claim: China's rising DRAM supplier CXMT posted $8 billion in 2025 revenue but sells primarily in China and lags industry leaders on specs, while Yangtze Memory is building three new factories to double capacity.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Chinese memory capacity additions are a medium-term oversupply and share-loss risk for the incumbent three, mostly in commodity/domestic segments.
insights.locator: There is a rising DRAM supplier in China

insights.claim: The critical shortage is in HBM DRAM required by all GPUs/XPUs from Nvidia, AMD, Google and Amazon, with about 80% of HBM made in South Korea.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: HBM is the true memory chokepoint and its 80% concentration in South Korea layers supply risk on top of geopolitical risk for every accelerator.
insights.quote: About 80% is made in South Korea.
insights.locator: The critical shortage is in HBM

insights.claim: HBM is very hard to manufacture because it requires both DRAM wafer fab capacity and complex packaging of 8-, 12-, or 16-high DRAM stacks with through-vias, packing ever more memory into a small space.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: The dual fab-plus-advanced-packaging requirement explains why HBM supply cannot ramp quickly, sustaining scarcity and margin for leaders like SK hynix.
insights.locator: Manufacturing more HBM requires DRAM wafer fab capacity

insights.claim: DRAM's historically brutal boom-and-bust cycle makes the three makers conservative about adding capacity, and because a new DRAM process takes over five years to develop and a fab several more years, supply cannot respond quickly.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: 5+ year development lead times plus cycle-scarred caution mean the memory shortage and elevated pricing likely persist well into the AI buildout.
insights.quote: a new DRAM process takes >5 years to develop, and a fab several years
insights.locator: DRAM has traditionally been a brutal boom and bust business

insights.claim: Unlike TSMC, DRAM companies are raising prices to meet demand and signing strategic supply agreements, such as one between Micron and Anthropic, to lock in long-term demand typically with upfront cash, helping push all three makers above $1 trillion market cap.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Upfront-cash long-term supply deals (Micron-Anthropic) de-risk memory-maker revenue and validate the trillion-dollar re-rating of DRAM equities.
insights.quote: all three companies are now >$1 trillion market cap
insights.locator: Unlike TSMC, DRAM companies are raising prices

insights.claim: JEDEC recently approved a new HBM version using more standard packages and glass substrates that can avoid the ABF shortage, but this will take years to phase in.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: A glass-substrate HBM standard is a long-dated fix for the ABF chokepoint and a tailwind for glass-substrate suppliers, not near-term relief.
insights.locator: JEDEC recently approved a new version of HBM

insights.claim: Power is the biggest problem for data centers, named by Amazon's CEO as the #1 constraint, and operators are sourcing it from grid power, natural gas, Bloom Energy fuel cells, Babcock & Wilcox steam turbines, and solar-plus-batteries.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: With power the CEO-declared #1 constraint, behind-the-meter power suppliers (Bloom Energy, gas turbines) become critical enablers and investment targets.
insights.quote: The biggest problem for data centers is Power.
insights.locator: Bottleneck #3 – Data Centers, especially Power

insights.claim: The grid cannot add capacity fast enough for data-center demand, and there is growing community resistance in many locations to data centers raising local electricity prices.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Grid inelasticity plus local political pushback pushes AI power off-grid and slows siting, raising the value of self-generation and existing interconnects.
insights.locator: The grid can't add capacity fast enough

insights.claim: Tesla, Sunrun, and Renew Home (a Google spin-off) announced a collaboration to free up electrical capacity for 17 large data centers during peak demand by getting consumers to opt in and let their home batteries be tapped.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Virtual-power-plant / distributed home-battery schemes are emerging as a demand-response supply source for AI data centers, a new market for Tesla and Sunrun.
insights.locator: Three companies — Tesla, Sunrun & Renew Home

insights.claim: Chevron struck a 20-year agreement to sell electricity to Microsoft, working with Joulent, which is building a 2.7GW generation plant in the heart of the Permian Basin in West Texas where gas supply exceeds pipeline takeaway.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Locating 2.7GW of gas generation at stranded-gas wellheads is a scalable template pairing oil majors with hyperscalers, bypassing the grid entirely.
insights.quote: Chevron recently struck a 20-year agreement to sell electricity to Microsoft working with Joulent, which is building a 2.7GW power generation plant
insights.locator: Hyperscalers are contracting natural gas

insights.claim: Beyond power sources, ancillary electrical gear is in short supply, and GE Vernova, the leading gas turbine supplier, is sold out through 2029.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: GE Vernova sold out through 2029 plus transformer/breaker shortages means power-equipment makers have multi-year backlogs and pricing power.
insights.quote: GE Vernova, the leading gas turbine supplier, is sold out through 2029.
insights.locator: It's not just the power sources in short supply

insights.claim: Solar and batteries alone are uneconomic for data centers because the worst-case winter cloudy day requires capital multiples of a sunny summer day, so they will be paired with natural gas, especially when gas or turbines are scarce.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: The winter-worst-case economics cap standalone renewables for 24/7 AI loads and entrench natural gas as the base-load partner for data-center power.
insights.locator: Solar and batteries are components of an off-grid power solution

insights.claim: Most Texans oppose data center construction over concerns it will drive up electricity prices and use scarce water, though Cerebras CEO Andrew Feldman notes the California almond industry uses more water than all US data centers combined.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Public opposition even in Texas is a real siting risk, but the almond-water comparison suggests water fears may be overstated relative to the power constraint.
insights.quote: the California almond industry uses more water than all the data centers in the USA
insights.locator: There is also growing resistance

insights.claim: Lasers used today in scale-out pluggable optical transceivers will also be used in scale-up co-packaged optics (CPO) links becoming prevalent over the next 2 to 5 years, and there are 10 to 100x more scale-up links than scale-out links.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: The 10-100x scale-up link multiplier means CPO adoption creates an order-of-magnitude larger laser TAM, a structural demand step-change for InP suppliers.
insights.quote: There are 10 to 100X more scale-up links than scale-out.
insights.locator: Lasers will also be used in scale-up CPO

insights.claim: The three largest laser suppliers, Coherent, Lumentum, and Sumitomo, together hold 68% share, with Lumentum the largest and Coherent the first to move to 6-inch InP (Indium Phosphide) wafers.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: A concentrated 68% top-three share plus Coherent's 6-inch InP lead identifies the direct beneficiaries of the optical-interconnect buildout.
insights.quote: The largest 3 laser suppliers are Coherent, Lumentum, and Sumitomo, which together have 68% share
insights.locator: The largest 3 laser suppliers

insights.claim: AI laser leaders Lumentum and Coherent are each now over $60 billion market cap, roughly 10x their value a year ago, and both are sold out and require upfront cash to secure capacity.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: A 10x re-rating with sold-out, cash-upfront capacity shows the market already pricing in the optical demand surge; watch InP capacity execution.
insights.quote: AI laser leaders Lumentum and Coherent are now over $60 billion market cap each, 10X what they were just a year ago.
insights.locator: AI laser leaders Lumentum and Coherent

insights.claim: In March, Nvidia announced a $2 billion investment in each of Lumentum and Coherent to secure supply-chain capacity, shortly before GTC where Jensen Huang showed CPO starting on the Nvidia roadmap from 2028.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Nvidia putting $2B into each laser leader and roadmapping CPO from 2028 is a strong signal to co-invest ahead of the optical transition.
insights.quote: In March, Nvidia announced a $2 billion investment in each of them to secure supply chain capacity.
insights.locator: AI laser leaders Lumentum and Coherent

insights.claim: Coherent's InP output capacity doubled in 2026 and will more than double in 2027, while Lumentum is rapidly growing InP capacity, yet demand is growing even faster than supply.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Even with capacity more than doubling, demand outpacing supply supports sustained laser pricing but flags this as the most manageable of the four bottlenecks.
insights.quote: its InP output capacity doubled in 2026, and will more than double in 2027
insights.locator: Coherent also did an investor briefing at OFC

insights.claim: The laser bottleneck is the most manageable of the big four because more suppliers, smaller InP fab CapEx, and shorter fab lead times make optical capacity easier to add than foundry, memory, or power.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Ranking lasers as the easiest bottleneck to solve helps prioritize where scarcity-driven pricing power is most durable (foundry, memory, power) versus transient.
insights.quote: That means this bottleneck is the most manageable of the big four bottlenecks.
insights.locator: The more suppliers, the smaller CapEx

insights.claim: TSMC's capacity is primarily in Taiwan, immediately adjacent to a much larger China that has the world's second-largest military and a stated intention to reunify Taiwan by force if necessary.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: Concentration of AI-compute manufacturing in Taiwan is the single largest tail risk to the entire AI supply chain; a hedge/geographic-diversification thesis.
insights.locator: GeoPolitical Risks for Foundry and DRAM

insights.claim: South Korea makes 80% of the world's HBM, but all its fabs and packaging factories are within missile and nuclear range of North Korea, described as the world's least predictable country.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: 80% of HBM being within North Korean strike range compounds Taiwan risk, meaning a single regional conflict could sever both compute and memory supply.
insights.quote: all of the fabs and packaging factories are within range of the missiles and nuclear weapons of the world's least predictable country, North Korea
insights.locator: South Korea makes 80% of the world's HBM

insights.claim: Rapid AI growth is stressing numerous parts of the supply chain and, although the supply chain is responding, these bottlenecks are likely to persist for years.
insights.speaker: Geoff Tate
insights.so_what: A multi-year bottleneck thesis argues for durable pricing power across chokepoint suppliers rather than a near-term supply normalization.
insights.quote: these bottlenecks are likely to be with us for years
insights.locator: Conclusion
